category_title: Social students with LD who frequently seek teacher assistance to initiate and sustain writing tasks
minimal_context: Julia is working on an expository essay about her favorite holiday during a thirty-minute writing class and needs to expand her details past the first paragraph.
dialogue:
TUTOR: You have thirty minutes of writing time today. Since you finished your first paragraph about your favorite holiday, please write the next paragraph and add more details.

STUDENT: I wrote my first paragraph: 'Everybody has a favorite holiday . Mine is easter. I like to paint the eggs . I like to find the egg’s to. that’s what I like about Easter.' But I'm stuck now.

TUTOR: What do you think you should write about next?

STUDENT: I don't know. I need you to help me figure out what to say next. I can't move further without help.

TUTOR: Just keep trying. Write down whatever comes to mind to make the essay longer.

STUDENT: (Puts her pencil down and raises her hand) I can't get started on the next part by myself. I need to talk through it with you and get help with my spelling and sentences before I can go back to work.

options.A: Use "active ignoring" of the student's minor misbehaviors as a consequence-based tool.
options.B: Talk through her areas of trouble and provide direct help with her writing.
options.C: Instruct the student to just rephrase the paragraph into her own words.
options.D: Acknowledge and praise appropriate student behaviors (e.g., "It's awesome to see your focus").
